message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 30 (15 todo)
Recent Green phase: No code change — "dragon sword ench 8 → 400" was a confirming test. (Note: across the spec, dragon material always yields full reimbursement, which equals the standard no-clause path; only the high-enchantment ≥8 clause reduces payout, and it wins when both apply. So the dragon clause never diverges from standard in any example and needs no explicit code.)

Refactor while keeping all tests green. No implementation changed this cycle. Evaluate naming and Four Rules briefly; if nothing warranted, document why and return.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by measuring the current state of the code.
Now let me run the pre-measurement: ESLint for smells and cognitive complexity, plus tests for baseline.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>/dev/null; echo "---EXIT:$?---"
input.description: Run ESLint to get smells and cognitive complexity
Run tests to establish green baseline

content.text:
PRE baseline:
- ESLint smells: 0 (clean — no violations whatsoever)
- Cognitive complexity: nothing reported above SonarJS threshold
- Tests: 30/30 passing, 15 todo

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for the functions. The recent Green phase added no code (a confirming test for "dragon sword ench 8 → 400"). The relevant function for that test is `damageReimbursement`. Let me compute metrics for the functions most affected by the claim path.

**McCabe cyclomatic complexity** (per function, start at 1):

- `componentGroupBase`: 1 + ternary = **2**
- `itemSurchargeRate`: 1 + two ternaries = **3**
- `isHighlyEnchanted`: 1 + `??` = **2**
- `componentBaseSum`: 1 + for-loop + `??` = **3**
- `damageReimbursement`: 1 + `&&` + ternary + `??` = **4**
- `damagePayout`: 1 = **1**
- `processClaim`: 1 = **1**
- `processQuote`: 1 + comparison (`> 0`) = **2**
- `runScenario`: 1 + ternary = **2**

Worst McCabe: `damageReimbursement` at **4**.

**APP mass for `damageReimbursement`** (the function tied to this cycle's confirming test):

```
const damageReimbursement = (item, amount) =>
  item && (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_THRESHOLD
    ? HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_RATE * amount
    : amount;
```
- Constants: 1 (`0`) → 1
- Bindings: `item`, `amount`, `item.enchantment`, `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_RATE`, `amount`, `amount` → 7
- Invocations: `&&`, `??`, `>=`, `*` → 4 invocations → 8
- Conditionals: ternary → 4
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Total: 1 + 7 + 8 + 4 = **20**

**Naming evaluation** for `damageReimbursement`: The name describes computing the reimbursable amount for a damaged item, applying the high-enchantment reduction. Based on all tests (dragon full, high-ench 50%, standard full), the name accurately reveals intent — it returns the reimbursement before deductible. The companion `damagePayout` applies the deductible. The naming pair is clear and accurate.

**Four Rules assessment:**

- **Rule 1 (Tests pass):** 30/30 green. ✅
- **Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** Names are domain-specific and accurate. `mainItems`, `componentBaseSum`, `damageReimbursement`, `findInsuredItem` all read clearly. No misleading names.
- **Rule 3 (No duplication):** The discount helpers (`loyaltyDiscount`/`followUpDiscount`) already funnel through `policyDiscount`. Surcharge logic is centralized in `withSurcharge`. No meaningful duplication.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Each constant and helper is referenced. `damageReimbursement`/`damagePayout` are correctly separated (reimbursement vs. deductible application). No dead abstractions.

This cycle changed no implementation — it was a confirming test. The code is already at a clean state across all four measurements.

Let me verify there is genuinely nothing to gain by checking whether `damageReimbursement`'s `&&` guard could be simplified. The `item` undefined-guard exists because `findInsuredItem` can return `undefined`. Removing it would change behavior for the error-handling todos (claim referencing item not in policy). It is load-bearing, not removable noise.

**No refactoring performed** — here is the full PRE/POST evidence trail (POST equals PRE because no change was warranted).

**Refactoring Evaluation (worst-offender target: `damageReimbursement`):**

| Metric | PRE | POST | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells (file) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Cognitive complexity (any fn over threshold) | none | none | — |
| APP mass (`damageReimbursement`) | 20 | 20 | 0 |
| McCabe (`damageReimbursement`, worst) | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| Tests | 30/30 | 30/30 | green |

**Reasoning for no change:**
- ESLint reports zero smells across the file — no nesting, line-length, param-count, or duplicate-string violations.
- The worst McCabe is 4 (`damageReimbursement`), driven by three genuine, irreducible decisions: the undefined-item guard (`&&`), the enchantment default (`??`), and the high-ench branch (ternary). Each maps to a distinct rule in the spec; collapsing any of them would either remove behavior or hide intent (violating Rule 2, which trumps APP/McCabe).
- APP mass of 20 is at the floor for a two-rule reimbursement calculation.
- Naming is accurate against all current tests; `damageReimbursement` vs `damagePayout` correctly separates the reimbursement-before-deductible from the deductible-applied amount.
- This cycle added no implementation (confirming test for the dragon+high-ench overlap), so there is no fresh code to clean up.

Any change here would trade clarity for a lower number with no net benefit. Code is already clean.

```
🔄 Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: none — code already clean across all four metrics
Smell delta: 0 → 0
Cognitive delta (worst fn): below threshold → below threshold
APP mass delta (damageReimbursement): 20 → 20
McCabe delta (damageReimbursement, worst): 4 → 4
Tests: 30/30 passing ✅
```

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_00-18-11_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

No changes made this cycle. All 30 tests remain green; ESLint reports zero smells; the worst-case function (`damageReimbursement`, McCabe 4 / APP mass 20) is at its complexity floor for the spec behavior it expresses, and its naming accurately reveals intent against all current tests.
